findings.description: Pagination is done by Python list slicing on the full result set instead of SQL LIMIT/OFFSET, so the entire expensive fetch+filter+sort pipeline runs on every page request regardless of which page is asked for.

SELECT ... fetchall() pulls every non-deleted (and optionally per-department) user row into Python with no LIMIT, turning the database into a dumb bucket and making cost scale with total table size rather than page size.
Materializes a fresh dict for every fetched row before any filtering, allocating N dicts to ultimately keep only ~page_size of them.

Regex name filter runs in Python over every row instead of being pushed into SQL (LIKE/GLOB/REGEXP), round-tripping every display_name through the interpreter, including rows that are immediately discarded.
Sorts the entire result set in Python (O(N log N) with a per-element .lower() in the key lambda) when ORDER BY ... COLLATE NOCASE / LOWER() could be done in the indexed SQL query.
total is computed as len() of the fully materialized Python list, which only works because all rows were over-fetched; with proper LIMIT/OFFSET this should be a separate SELECT COUNT(*).
Every public method opens and closes a fresh sqlite3 connection with no reuse/pooling, paying repeated file-open and page-cache warmup under bursty call patterns.

Query performance for department/email/deleted predicates depends entirely on indexes (incl. a partial index on deleted=0) that are not visible in this module's scope; flagged so schema owners verify they exist.

Off-by-one pagination: offset = page * page_size with 1-based page means page 1 skips the first page_size rows (shows rows 20-39), making the first page unreachable; should be (page - 1) * page_size — defer detailed correctness review to critic-logic.
